rulings.Case Title: LUIS SALAZAR VS. KAEL FOODS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Class Action Settlement
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion/Discovery Calendar for February 18, 2025, line 2. PLAINTIFF LUIS SALAZAR'S Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Class Action Settlement. Hearing required. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: RAYMOND VINCENT DIGIACOMO JR VS. SHRYNE GROUP, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Amend Second Amended Complaint; Declaration; And, Memorandum.
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion/Discovery Calendar for February 18, 2025, line 13. PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO'S Motion To Amend Second Amended Complaint. The matter is continued for hearing to March 10, 2025. On January 7, 2025, the court granted the motion of defendants Shryne Group, Inc., Shryne USA LLC, Stiizy, LLC, and Stiizy Development LLC to require plaintiff to furnish security as a vexatious litigant to proceed with the litigation; plaintiff's deadline was February 10, 2025. As to remaining defendant Vigilant Security, the court granted its motion requiring plaintiff to furnish security on February 3, 2025, and plaintiff's deadline to do so is March 3, 2025. Plaintiff here seeks to amend the complaint as to all of these defendants; the court continues hearing on the matter until after the deadline to furnish security as to Vigilant so that the court can ascertain what claims, if any, may proceed. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. rulings.Case Title: DANISHIA DAVID VS. CHINATOWN COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT CENTER, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set Two And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion/Discovery Calendar for February 18, 2025, line 9. DEFENDANTS MALCOLM YEUNG, CHINATOWN COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT CENTER, INC'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set Two And Request For Sanctions. Defendant's unopposed motion to compel further responses to Special Interrogatories, Set Two, and for sanctions is granted. Plaintiff failed to respond to the interrogatories timely and has thereby waived objections. Plaintiff is ordered to respond to Special Interrogatories, Set Two, Nos. 69-104, within 20 days of notice of entry of this order. Defendant further seeks sanctions. Sanctions are mandatory unless the court finds that the party unsuccessfully opposing the motion acted with substantial justification or other circumstances render the sanction unjust. The court does not so find and orders Plaintiff to pay $2500 to Defendant within 20 days of notice of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. rulings.Case Title: CHLOE BALZER VS. NEAL MARGULIS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave Of Court To Conduct Financial Discovery On Defendants Neal Margulis, An Individual: And Digital Display Innovations, Llc; A California Limited Liability Company
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion/Discovery Calendar for February 18, 2025, line 10. PLAINTIFF CHLOE BALZER'S Motion For Leave Of Court To Conduct Financial Discovery On Defendants Neal Margulis, and Digital Display Innovations, LLC. The unopposed motion of plaintiff Chloe Balzer for punitive damages discovery against defendants Neal Margulis and Digital Display Innovations LLC is denied without prejudice. Civil Code 3295(c) regulates punitive damages discovery. It states that the court may order such discovery if "the plaintiff has established that there is a substantial probability that the plaintiff will prevail on the claim pursuant to Section 3294." This is an evidentiary showing that must be based on "supporting and opposing affidavits" or other factual showing. Plaintiff supports her request with an attorney declaration that does not show personal knowledge, and with attachments containing hearsay. On its face plaintiff's showing does not establish that she will prevail on the claim pursuant to Section 3294. Accordingly, the court denies her request without prejudice. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. rulings.Case Title: JPMORGAN CHASE BANK, N.A. VS. FRIDA N GILCHRIST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order That Matters In Request For Admission Of Truth Of Facts Be Deemed Admitted;
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion/Discovery Calendar for February 18, 2025, line 4. PLAINTIFF JPMORGAN CHASE BANK, N.A.'S Motion For Order That Matters In Request For Admission Of Truth Of Facts Be Deemed Admitted. JP Morgan Chase Bank N.A.'s unopposed motion to deem requests for admissions admitted is granted. The truth of any matters specified in the requests is deemed admitted in light of responding party's failure to respond to the requests for admissions before the date of hearing. See CCP 2033.280(b). CCP 2033.280(c) states that a court order for sanctions is mandatory in this circumstance. The court orders defendant Frida N. Gilchrist to pay $50 to JP Morgan Chase, by payment to its counsel, within 20 court days of the date of notice of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: MARY-ANN KUBAL VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Punitive Damages Allegations And Request For Punitive Damages In Plaintiff?S Complaint As To Defendant Christrian Alberto Figueroa
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion/Discovery Calendar for February 18, 2025, line 7. DEFENDANT CHRISTIAN FIGUEROA'S Motion To Strike Punitive Damages Allegations And Request For Punitive Damages. Defendant Christian Alberto's motion to strike plaintiff Mary-Ann Kubal's punitive damages allegations is denied. Kubal alleges that "she was an Uber passenger, riding home to her apartment in San Francisco, California. Her Uber driver was Defendant Christian Alberto Figueroa. Mr. Figueroa was driving under the influence of alcohol and/or drugs. He was operating the vehicle recklessly and crashed into a light pole, causing severe and life-threatening injuries to Mary-Ann Kubal, as set forth more fully below." (Compl. para. 1.) Alberto contends that Kubal's factual showing is insufficient because it does not specify the alleged level of intoxication or other additional facts, such as a particular pattern of reckless driving, that would make punitive damages appropriate. To the extent Alberto claims Kubal's pleading is insufficiently specific, the court finds otherwise; Kubal is required to plead only the ultimate fact of intoxication and not the evidentiary facts that she may use at trial to support the ultimate fact. (See Clauson v. Superior Court (1998) 67 Cal.App.4th 1253, 1255 ["In order to survive a motion to strike an allegation of punitive damages, the ultimate facts showing an entitlement to such relief must be pled by a plaintiff."].) Alberto also contends that the ultimate fact of driving while intoxicated is insufficient to support punitive damages. This is incorrect; the Supreme Court held otherwise in Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, 892-893. The case on which Alberto relies, Dawes v. Superior Court (1980) 111 Cal.App.3d 82, 90, is not to the contrary; Dawes considered whether to apply the new categorical rule announced in Taylor retroactively but ultimately did not reach the question because it found that the allegations of the plaintiff before it were sufficient to state a claim for punitive damages even under pre-Taylor authority. In any event, Dawes (a decision of the California Court of Appeal) could not overrule the California Supreme Court's decision in Taylor. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California-certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)
